title: 4  Mixture-of-Experts, Efficient Architectures, and the 2026 Landscape [F+S]
text:
4.17 Notes and Sources
The conditional-computation lineage starts with Shazeer et al., Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer, followed here by GShard, Switch Transformers, and ST-MoE. Switch motivates top-1 routing and its auxiliary balance objective; ST-MoE develops stability guidance including router z-loss. The DeepSeek-V3 technical report is the primary source for its total/active counts, auxiliary-loss-free balancing, MLA-plus-MoE architecture, and multi-token prediction objective. Exact fixture fields come from the linked official configs.
For the fixed-state lineage, see S4, Mamba, Mamba-2 and structured state-space duality, Gated Delta Networks, and Kimi Linear. The chapter uses their architectural contracts and lineage, not their paper-specific benchmark claims.
For learned sparse attention, see Native Sparse Attention, Mixture of Block Attention, and the DeepSeek-V3.2 report for DSA. These methods differ in granularity and selector design; grouping them does not imply identical complexity or quality.
For alternative decoding and supervision, see Gloeckle et al., Better & Faster Large Language Models via Multi-token Prediction, and the Mercury technical report. Current product claims are confined to dated Landscape 2026 boxes so the mechanism chapter remains valid when the frontier changes.

5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
section: 5.3 The Budget Fails Before Training Starts
text:
5.3 The Budget Fails Before Training Starts
A team has enough accelerator time for one base-model run. The first proposal is a three-billion-parameter decoder trained on twenty billion tokens. The second doubles the parameters and halves the tokens. Both use roughly the same dense-Transformer training compute. Neither proposal states the expected loss, whether the dataset contains enough unique text, how much of that text survives rights and quality gates, or how the team will notice a bad data mixture before the budget is gone.
This is first a model-and-data allocation problem. Parameter count N controls one source of error; training-token count D controls another; the token distribution determines what loss means. A run can be compute-optimal for one distribution yet commercially poor, repeat low-quality data, or hide a rare-language, code, or factual regression behind smooth aggregate loss.
A scaling law is an empirical response surface over model size, data, and sometimes other resource axes. A pretraining data pipeline produces the D entering that surface. The fit is conditional on architecture, optimizer, tokenizer, corpus, and measurement protocol; treating it as physics while treating data as file preparation creates false precision.
You will fit a joint law to a proxy ladder, derive the compute-optimal allocation, and attach an interval to a tenfold extrapolation. Then a synthetic WET-style corpus passes extraction, rights and quality gates, near-deduplication, decontamination, and mixture selection before paired Chapter 2 models compare raw with cleaned text. The laptop result is not a frontier forecast; it makes assumptions inspectable before scale makes them expensive.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.4 What you will build






Tip



The chapter artifact. scaling_fit.py fits

L(N,D)=E+A\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right)^{-\alpha}
       +B\left(\frac{D}{D_0}\right)^{-\beta}

to twelve synthetic proxy runs. scaling_model.py solves the compute-constrained optimum; scaling_uncertainty.py residual-bootstraps fit and predictive intervals. The data path separates extraction and policy, deduplication, and mixture logic into data_records.py, deduplication.py, and mixture.py. The thin run_build.py composes them, imports Chapter 2’s BPE and TinyGPT, generates about 50 MiB of synthetic text, and writes plotted values to CSV or JSON.
Success means the fit recovers the proxy generator’s parameter and data exponents, the optimum satisfies C=6ND, every document has a final decision, near duplicates share a cluster, the evaluation needle is absent, exact mixture quotas are inspectable, and the cleaned-data condition has lower loss on an independently worded held-out slice across paired initializations. The frozen tokenizer is calibrated on text independent of both conditions. The experiment is a mechanism test, not a frontier forecast, web-corpus release, or throughput benchmark.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.5 Power Laws You Can Fit
Language-model loss often improves smoothly with resources. Kaplan and colleagues documented approximate power laws: reducible loss falls as a resource raised to a negative exponent. For one resource x,

L(x)=L_\infty+Kx^{-\gamma},

where L_\infty is a fitted floor, K>0 is a scale coefficient, and \gamma>0 is an exponent. Subtract the floor and take logarithms:

\log\bigl(L(x)-L_\infty\bigr)=\log K-\gamma\log x.

This is linear in log-log coordinates only when the floor and regime fit. The slope is -\gamma; a small exponent means multiplying resources yields modest improvement.
Three qualifications matter. Omitting a nonzero floor bends the slope. A law is local to its architecture, tokenizer, data, optimizer, and regime. Resource axes interact: a parameter-only law fitted to varying token budgets confounds capacity with exposure. The build therefore fits

L(N,D)=E+A\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right)^{-\alpha}
       +B\left(\frac{D}{D_0}\right)^{-\beta}.

E is a fitted floor; A,B and \alpha,\beta are positive coefficients and exponents; N_0,D_0 are fixed reference units for numerical conditioning and do not change predictions.
The artifact constrains positive quantities in log space and constrains E below the smallest observed loss. Its public fit is deliberately small:
rows = load_observations(Path("fixtures/proxy_ladder.csv"))
law = fit_scaling_law(rows)

for row in rows:
    predicted = law.loss(row.parameters, row.tokens)
    residual = row.loss - predicted
Twelve observations are enough to exercise the mechanics, not enough to license a production budget. The proxy points were generated around \alpha=0.34 and \beta=0.28 with small deterministic perturbations. The fitted values are {0.332} and {0.287}. That agreement verifies the implementation against a known generator. It says nothing about the exponents of an unmeasured architecture or corpus.
Inspect residuals by model size and data ratio. Structure or growth at one end signals misspecification or regime change; low average error can still overstate larger models. Reserve whole ladder points for validation.
The artifact keeps the ladder fixed, resamples centered residuals, refits, and recomputes the optimum. Fitted-optimum quantiles form a fit interval; one additional sampled residual forms a predictive interval under an i.i.d. residual model. Neither covers distribution shift, failures, optimizer changes, or wrong functional form. Report uncertainty inside the law and scenarios about it.








Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




scaling_model.py
173 lines
positive coefficients and exponents fit both resource axes


scaling_uncertainty.py
64 lines
the 10× forecast carries a finite-ladder interval



The fitted surface is a compact summary of experiments. Its authority cannot exceed the ladder, controls, and held-out checks from which it was estimated.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
section: 5.6 Chinchilla Makes Size and Data a Joint Decision
text:
5.6 Chinchilla Makes Size and Data a Joint Decision
Hoffmann and colleagues revisited allocation under fixed compute and found parameters and data should grow together more nearly than earlier work implied. The durable contribution is the IsoFLOP experiment, not one token ratio: hold compute fixed, vary N,D, and find minimum loss.
For a dense decoder-only Transformer, a useful first-order training estimate is

C\approx 6ND,

where C is floating-point operations, N is non-embedding trainable parameters used per token, and D is training tokens seen. The factor six approximates a forward pass plus gradients with respect to weights and activations. It is not a device invoice. Attention terms, embeddings, sparsity, sequence length, recomputation, padding, optimizer work, and kernel efficiency produce deviations. Use measured step FLOPs when available and state which parameter ledger an MoE uses; Chapter 4 explains why total stored parameters are not the correct active-compute count.
The Chinchilla paper used three complementary approaches:

Train several fixed model sizes for different durations, then infer the lower envelope of loss over compute.
At several fixed compute budgets, fit the IsoFLOP curve over model size and locate its minimum.
Fit the global parametric surface L(N,D) across all runs, then solve its compute-constrained optimum.

Agreement reduces dependence on one fit; disagreement is diagnostic. Because finite-run noise and fitting procedure bias the apparent optimum, reproduce at least a global surface and held-out IsoFLOP profiles.
The optimum of the joint form can be derived. Define normalized variables n=N/N_0, d=D/D_0, and normalized compute c=C/(6N_0D_0). The constraint is nd=c, so d=c/n. Ignoring the constant floor, minimize

f(n)=An^{-\alpha}+Bc^{-\beta}n^{\beta}.

Set the derivative to zero:

-\alpha A n^{-\alpha-1}
+\beta Bc^{-\beta}n^{\beta-1}=0.

Therefore

n^*=\left(\frac{\alpha A}{\beta B}c^\beta\right)^{1/(\alpha+\beta)},
\qquad
d^*=\frac{c}{n^*}.

Model size scales with compute as C^{\beta/(\alpha+\beta)} and tokens as C^{\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)}. With similarly sized exponents, both grow approximately as the square root of compute. The build’s tenfold extrapolation chooses approximately {2.21} billion parameters and {79.2} billion tokens, satisfying {6ND=1.05\times10^{21}} FLOPs. Its predicted synthetic loss is {2.223}, with a 5th-to-95th percentile residual-bootstrap predictive interval of [2.220,2.225]. Again, this tight conditional interval reflects a clean synthetic generator; a real planning interval should include broader scenarios.
Compute must become wall time before it becomes a schedule. If P_\text{peak} is peak FLOP/s per accelerator, G is accelerator count, and u is measured model FLOP utilization (MFU), then

t_\text{wall}\approx\frac{C}{G\,u\,P_\text{peak}}.

MFU is configuration-specific. Using peak throughput silently assumes u=1. Record the FLOP convention, measured MFU range, checkpoint and retry overhead, and a sensitivity table; Chapter 6 handles execution mechanics.






Figure 5.1: The left panel checks fitted versus observed loss for twelve synthetic proxy runs. The right panel holds compute at ten times the largest proxy run, varies model size, and marks the fitted optimum with a 90 percent residual-bootstrap predictive interval. Data are synthetic and generated locally.



The curve’s U shape is the decision. Too small a model spends the budget repeatedly exposing insufficient capacity; too large a model spends it on parameters that do not receive enough data. The minimum belongs to a training objective and budget, not automatically to deployment.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
section: 5.7 Scaling Laws Are Decision Tools, Not Deployment Commandments
text:
5.7 Scaling Laws Are Decision Tools, Not Deployment Commandments
“Chinchilla-optimal” asks which N,D allocation minimizes fitted loss under fixed training compute; it does not minimize an agent product’s lifetime cost. With large serving demand, a smaller model trained longer can cost more once and save on every request. Inference-aware scaling therefore adds deployment demand, latency, batch shape, hardware, quantization, caching, and quality value to the objective.
Intentional overtraining means using more tokens than a chosen loss-optimal baseline, not necessarily harmful optimization. A small, long-lived model may justify extra training because weight and memory costs recur during serving. Yet ordinary-regime ratios may extrapolate poorly to thousands of tokens per parameter. Run overtraining ladders rather than promoting a ratio to law.
Other constraints change the response surface:








Constraint or method
Additional axis
Durable planning implication




Limited unique data
reuse count and source diversity
several repeats can be useful, but marginal value falls; track unique and tokens-seen separately


Lower numerical precision
training precision and post-training quantization
precision can change effective capacity and optimization; an overtrained checkpoint may be more quantization-sensitive


Distillation
teacher quality, teacher cost, student count
an existing teacher or many students can justify distillation; training one teacher solely for one student changes the comparison


Mixture of experts
active parameters, total experts, selected experts
active compute and stored capacity need separate laws; gains from adding experts can saturate


Inference demand
lifetime served tokens and latency
the training-loss optimum can differ from the deployment-economic optimum



These laws are empirical families, not algebraic add-ons to Chinchilla. A precision law fitted to one bit-width schedule or a distillation law fitted to one teacher family is a prior for experiment design. It is not a transferable constant. For MoE, fit active compute and expert count as separate variables if the program needs that decision; this chapter does not hide a second MoE fit inside the dense artifact.

5.7.1 Apparent emergence
Some benchmark curves appear flat and then jump at a model scale, producing claims of emergent capability. A genuine phase change is possible, but the metric can manufacture one. Suppose the underlying probability assigned to a correct answer rises smoothly. Exact-match accuracy remains zero until the correct string becomes the top choice; multiple-choice accuracy changes only when a ranking flips; pass/fail thresholds discretize a continuous score. Schaeffer and colleagues demonstrated that changing to continuous metrics can turn apparent discontinuities into smooth improvement.
The operational response is not to deny every threshold. Preserve continuous observables—answer log likelihood, margin over alternatives, calibration, partial credit, verifier score, and task success by difficulty—alongside headline accuracy. Then a launch threshold can remain discrete while the learning curve stays diagnosable. If a capability matters for safety, do not assume that a flat thresholded metric proves absence below the observed jump.


5.7.2 New resource axes
Pretraining compute is no longer the sole scaling axis. Reinforcement-learning compute can improve a policy after base pretraining, and test-time compute can trade more sampled or deliberative work for better answers. Chapter 8 derives reasoning and test-time allocation; Chapter 23 owns agent-policy RL. In a full product budget, the axes interact: pretraining creates representations, post-training shapes behavior, inference-time search extracts more performance, and serving demand prices every extra token. Keep their ledgers separate before optimizing the portfolio.
A scaling fit earns trust through interventions: change one axis, hold the comparison contract stable, predict a withheld point, and inspect residuals. The law is useful when it can reject a bad allocation before the expensive run—not when it merely explains completed runs.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
section: 5.8 Base-Model Evaluation While the Run Is Alive
text:
5.8 Base-Model Evaluation While the Run Is Alive
A base model is a next-token predictor, not an instruction-following product. A chat wrapper confounds pretraining quality with templates, post-training, sampling, and defaults. Pretraining measurements must be repeatable, checkpoint-comparable, and sliced to expose distribution failures.
Cross-entropy is the first instrument. For target tokens x_1,\ldots,x_T and model probabilities p_\theta, the mean negative log likelihood is

\mathcal{L}=-\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\log p_\theta(x_t\mid x_{<t}),
\qquad
\operatorname{PPL}=e^{\mathcal{L}}.

Perplexity is exponentiated token-average surprise. Compare it only under the same tokenizer, normalization, context and document-boundary policy, and corpus: changing segmentation changes the averaging unit. Across tokenizers, add a byte or character denominator such as bits per byte and document normalization.
Aggregate loss can hide incidents. Maintain immutable slices for web prose, code, mathematics, target languages, long documents, sensitive tails, and trusted sources; report token-weighted and per-slice values. In-flight evaluation runs while training continues and asks whether optimization is healthy, the mixture produces intended capabilities, and checkpoints remain inside the fitted learning curve’s uncertainty.
A useful checkpoint contract includes:

training and held-out cross-entropy with exact token counts;
per-domain and per-language losses on versioned, contamination-audited slices;
gradient norm, update norm, learning rate, token throughput, and non-finite-event counters;
a compact set of conditional-likelihood tasks, with prompt text and few-shot examples pinned;
the residual between observed loss and the scaling-law prediction at actual N,D,C;
a checkpoint, tokenizer, data-manifest, code, and evaluator version that reconstructs every number.

The few-shot harness should score candidates by conditional log likelihood rather than sample one answer and hope decoding noise is representative. For prompt q and candidate completion a, compute

s(a\mid q)=\frac{1}{|a|}\sum_{t\in a}\log p_\theta(a_t\mid q,a_{<t}).

Length normalization prevents shorter candidates from winning automatically, but can change rankings. Pin separators, whitespace, labels, demonstrations and order, and continuation punctuation. MMLU- or HellaSwag-class names do not make a harness reproducible; documented setup sensitivity makes raw scores without configuration weak evidence.
The build’s five cloze items use two demonstrations, rotate answers across three positions, save first-seed completions, and report constant-position and shortest-candidate baselines. They cannot estimate general capability; they verify prompt construction, continuation scoring, baselines, and qualitative inspection. Chapter 22 supplies statistical evaluation; this section supplies its base-model telemetry.
Alert on trajectories, not one loss threshold. A mixture-boundary spike differs from a residual growing across checkpoints; a code gain with language forgetting is acceptable only if planned and recovered. Preserve the checkpoint and data window before changing controls. Exact token-interval replay is part of observability.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
section: 5.9 Data Is a Governed Supply Chain
text:
5.9 Data Is a Governed Supply Chain
D is not the size of a folder. It is the number of tokens the optimizer consumes after acquisition, extraction, eligibility policy, filtering, deduplication, mixture sampling, and repetition. A corpus ledger should distinguish at least raw bytes, extracted documents, eligible documents, near-unique tokens, mixture tokens, and tokens seen during training. Reporting “three trillion tokens” without the stage and tokenizer prevents comparison.
Common Crawl illustrates the transition. WARC stores responses and metadata, WAT computed metadata, and WET extracted plaintext. WET is convenient but has discarded structure and embedded extraction decisions. Retain WARC coordinates or equivalent locators for later quality, rights, and deletion work. Boilerplate removal, decoding, segmentation, language identification, and metadata preservation all alter the training distribution.
The reference Document keeps six fields because policy needs them:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Document:
    doc_id: str
    url: str
    source: str
    language: str
    rights: str
    text: str
A production record also needs crawl and extraction versions, retrieval time, hash, jurisdiction, declared license, contractual basis, robots and opt-out observations, PII class, transformations, parents, and deletion status. Do not collapse these into safe: crawler directives are not copyright licenses, public accessibility is not permission for every use, and an open-dataset wrapper does not guarantee uniform rights.
Rights policy varies by jurisdiction and contract. Store raw evidence, apply a versioned eligibility policy, record decision and reason, and keep descendants discoverable so removal can identify dependent extracts, synthetic transforms, mixtures, checkpoints, and adapters. Chapter 18 owns agent-memory deletion; the lineage principle begins here.
Privacy is connected to duplication: rare repeated strings receive more gradient exposure, and extraction risk grows with capacity, duplication, and context. Deduplication reduces waste and one memorization pressure, not all privacy risk. Apply explicit PII policy, restrict raw access, audit samples and exceptions, and measure extraction and redaction. A hashed email is still a stable identifier.
Acquisition creates representation errors: language ID rejects short or code-switched text, HTML tables become nonsense, PDF columns interleave, and deduplication clusters boilerplate. Audit every stage by source and language and preserve rejections. Counts show that a gate acted; samples show what it did.
The invariant is end-to-end lineage: every token admitted to a mixture has a source record, transformation history, policy decision, and reproducible text hash. This does not settle a legal question. It makes the organization’s decision inspectable and correctable.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.10 Filter, Deduplicate, and Decontaminate
Extraction maximizes recall; curation chooses what should train the model. A filter scores or rejects individual documents. Deduplication finds repeated or near-repeated content within or across sources. Decontamination checks training candidates against evaluation material. They solve different problems and should write different reason codes.
Early corpora such as C4 and Gopher used transparent heuristics for language, length, character ratios, punctuation, repetition, stop words, and blocked terms. They are cheap and debuggable but encode cultural assumptions. The C4 audit found identity-term blocklists disproportionately removed minority-associated documents; a mainstream-English quality score can erase dialect, community discussion, or rare languages.
Model filters rank documents using a learned quality concept. DCLM and FineWeb-Edu showed strong controlled results, but label source is policy: “encyclopedic” differs from “educational,” “reliable code,” or “serves target users.” Evaluator preferences can hide behind a score. Retain distributions, threshold sweeps, source-language curves, and reviewed false positives. The artifact intentionally implements only transparent features.

5.10.1 Near-duplicate mechanics
Exact hashes catch byte-identical records. Web duplicates often differ by a menu, timestamp, punctuation, or one inserted paragraph. Convert normalized text into a set of word shingles—overlapping sequences of, say, five words. For shingle sets A and B, Jaccard similarity is

J(A,B)=\frac{|A\cap B|}{|A\cup B|}.

Comparing every document pair is quadratic. MinHash builds a compact signature whose agreement probability estimates Jaccard similarity. For each random permutation-like hash h_k, record the minimum hashed shingle. Two documents with similarity s match one MinHash component with probability s. Locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) divides a signature into b bands of r rows. A pair becomes a candidate if any band matches, with approximate probability

P(\text{candidate}\mid s)=1-(1-s^r)^b.

More bands raise recall and candidate count; more rows sharpen the threshold and can miss paraphrases. The artifact samples bottom-k stable shingle hashes, uses LSH only for candidates, then confirms Jaccard before unioning clusters. At web scale, stream shingles, control bucket skew, and measure false positives and negatives. Semantic methods such as SemDeDup catch lexical misses but can collapse legitimate viewpoints; review by language and source.
Exemplar choice matters: first can be truncated and longest can preserve boilerplate. The lab selects the highest transparent quality score and records membership, similarity, and cluster size. Production may add trust, recency, rights, extraction quality, and canonical URL. Redundancy never justifies deleting lineage.
Dedup changes learning. Lee and colleagues found that duplicated sequences contribute to memorized generations and train-test overlap; deduplication can improve efficiency and evaluation validity. It also changes domain weights. If a reference paragraph appears on a thousand sites, raw frequency makes it a thousand times more influential. Removing copies changes the empirical distribution, not only the bill.


5.10.2 Evaluation decontamination
The lab normalizes whitespace and case, then removes long exact evaluation substrings. Production combines hashes, substrings or n-grams, canaries, and cautious semantic matching; keeps prompts, answers, rationales, and mirrors separate; and rechecks transformed and synthetic descendants. Paraphrases evade exact matching, while broad semantic detection can delete legitimate knowledge.
Do not tune on the only clean set. Maintain a private final slice, date-bounded or source-bounded holdouts, and contamination controls where deliberate leakage quantifies score inflation. Public benchmark performance is useful only with a contamination statement. If a benchmark is discovered in the training corpus after a run, label the score; do not retroactively call the data clean.






Figure 5.2: The left panel counts synthetic documents after extraction, rights and quality filters, near-deduplication, exact evaluation decontamination, and mixture selection. The right panel shows the number of singleton and two-document near-duplicate clusters. Values come from a deterministic 50 MiB WET-style fixture, not scraped web data.



The count drop is not proof of quality. The evidence is the paired ledger: stage counts, per-reason rejections, reviewed samples, cluster membership, and downstream equal-compute evaluation.

Invariant now verified

deduplication.py

5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.11 Mixtures, Fertility, and Curriculum
A curated corpus is still not a training distribution until source weights are chosen. Let domain i contain eligible documents and let p_i be its sampling probability, with \sum_i p_i=1. Sampling proportional to available tokens reproduces corpus volume. That is rarely the desired objective: crawl abundance overweights repeated commercial pages; high-quality books or code may be scarce; low-resource languages may disappear. Mixture weights are model hyperparameters with governance consequences. The lab converts weights to exact document quotas and raises when a source cannot supply its quota; it never silently fills a zero-weight or missing source from leftovers.
The corpus lineage shows changing experimental styles:








Release
Historical contribution
Comparison caution




C4
roughly 750 GB of cleaned English text from one Common Crawl snapshot for T5
bytes after one recipe; English-only and audited for filter bias


The Pile
825 GiB across 22 named English components
explicit heterogeneous domains and weights, not one web dump


RedPajama
V1 reproduced a seven-domain recipe; V2 exposed a much larger web pool plus quality and duplicate signals
distinguish the raw annotated pool from a selected training mixture


Dolma
a three-trillion-token English mixture with curation toolkit and intermediate analyses
released recipe and tools make ablations reconstructible


FineWeb / FineWeb-Edu
multi-snapshot web curation and a learned educational subset
exact token counts depend on tokenizer; classifier creates a specialized view


DCLM
a controlled testbed separating a huge raw pool, fixed training scales, evaluations, and selected baselines
distinguish pool size, filtered dataset, added domains, and tokens actually seen


Nemotron-CC
a 6.3-trillion-token release combining globally deduplicated original and synthetic tokens
quality and unique-token yield are a frontier; synthetic provenance remains part of the count



These sizes are not a leaderboard. Some report bytes, some tokenizer-specific tokens; some are raw pools, others deduplicated selections; training tokens can include repeats. The durable progress is methodological: named sources, reproducible transformations, quality signals, controlled ablations, and explicit mixtures.
DoReMi treats mixture selection as a group-robust optimization problem. A small proxy model learns domain weights that reduce excess loss relative to reference losses, then a larger model trains on the resulting mixture. RegMix trains many small proxy models on sampled mixtures and fits a regression from mixture weights to downstream performance. Both replace intuition-only weighting with experiments. Neither removes the transfer question: proxy rankings can change with model size, token budget, or evaluation target. Validate candidate weights at several budgets and on the integrated mixture.
Mixture objectives need a target. Minimize token-weighted validation loss and large web domains dominate. Minimize worst-domain excess loss and small difficult domains receive more weight. Optimize a downstream suite and benchmark selection becomes the objective. A production proposal should show a Pareto table: aggregate loss, critical-domain losses, language coverage, unique tokens, rights cost, and expected serving value. One scalar score hides who pays for the compromise.

5.11.1 Fertility is a compute allocation
Chapter 2’s byte-fallback BPE can encode every Unicode string, but coverage does not imply equal efficiency. Tokenizer fertility is the number of tokens used per word or per character. If language \ell has character count Q_\ell and fertility f_\ell tokens per character, its token allocation is

D_\ell=f_\ell Q_\ell.

Training and serving cost scale with tokens. A tokenizer trained mostly on English may require more byte-level pieces for Arabic or Hindi, giving those users less semantic content per context window and a higher cost per character. Word-based fertility is linguistically confounded because languages segment and inflect differently; report both tokens per Unicode character and tokens per whitespace word, plus task-relevant units such as code characters or document pages.
Do not “correct” high fertility only by reducing a language’s sampling probability. That saves tokens by withholding content. Options include retraining the shared tokenizer with representative text, allocating more training tokens, using byte- or character-aware architectures, and evaluating quality per unit of source content. Tokenizer changes break direct perplexity comparisons and checkpoint compatibility, so make this decision before the major run when possible.


5.11.2 Mid-training is staged base-model work
Broad pretraining need not flow directly into instruction tuning. Mid-training is a base-model stage that keeps a language-model objective while changing data quality, domains, formats, sequence lengths, or learning-rate schedule. Continued pretraining resumes from a checkpoint on a new or updated distribution. Annealing or cooldown is the optimizer intervention, usually lowering learning rate toward zero; it is not a synonym for high-quality data. A stage may combine both, but its controls should separate them.
A common program has broad pretraining, a high-quality or capability-targeted mixture, then a long-context stage that includes genuinely long documents while retaining shorter high-quality material. Simply changing a RoPE configuration does not teach long-document use; Chapter 3 owns positional extension. Long-context training needs length distribution, domain balance, and evaluations beyond a single needle task. Chapter 11 owns domain customization after a base model exists.
A microanneal branches a small, compute-matched run from a shared checkpoint: candidate source or mixture versus control, followed by targeted base-model evaluations. It is a screening experiment, not a miniature proof. Data-source utility can change rank with scale. Test promising sources at more than one small budget, include a web-only or incumbent-mixture control, and confirm winners inside the final mixture. Log both the data change and the learning-rate path so a gain is not attributed to the wrong intervention.






Figure 5.3: The left panel compares paired-seed TinyGPT training means on raw and cleaned synthetic text; shaded ranges span three seeds and horizontal lines mark mean loss on a disjoint clean-domain holdout. Dashed lines are raw and solid lines are cleaned, so condition survives grayscale. The right panel measures tokens per Unicode character for five short language samples under one independently calibrated English-leaning Chapter 2 BPE. These are local CPU toy measurements, not model or language benchmarks.



The two panels expose one shared rule: “one token” is not a content-neutral unit. Cleaning changes which examples a token represents; fertility changes how much source content fits in the token budget.

5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
section: 5.12 Synthetic Data at the Data Wall
text:
5.12 Synthetic Data at the Data Wall
“Synthetic data” combines mechanisms with different failure modes. Separate at least four:

A grounded transformation of an authentic document, such as reformatting, summarization, rephrasing, or question generation while retaining the parent.
Fresh generation from prompts, simulations, or procedural environments.
Generated data accepted only after an executable, retrieval, human, or stronger-model verifier.
Recursive self-consumption in which model outputs replace or overwhelm the authentic distribution across generations.

Shumailov and colleagues studied the fourth case and showed model collapse: indiscriminate recursive replacement can lose distribution tails and eventually distort the learned distribution. Rare modes are sampled less often, then appear still less often in the next generation. The feedback loop narrows support. “A model wrote this token” is not by itself the collapse condition. Gerstgrasser and colleagues reproduced collapse under replacement but found that accumulating generated data alongside the original real data avoided it in their studied settings. WRAP showed that purposeful synthetic rephrasing mixed with real web documents can improve efficiency.
The engineering rule is an authentic anchor, not a universal synthetic percentage. Preserve an immutable, provenance-tagged corpus from the target distribution and authentic held-out slices. Compare real-only, synthetic-only, and mixed conditions at equal compute. Track tail slices, diversity, duplication, calibration, and general-domain retention. Version generator checkpoint, prompt, sampler, parent document, verifier, acceptance threshold, and generation number. A synthetic descendant inherits rights and deletion questions from its parent; lineage should not stop at the generator.
Verification changes the failure, not necessarily removes it. An executable test can strongly validate code outputs inside its coverage. A learned judge can reward its own stylistic preferences. An ICLR 2026 study found that an external verifier prevented collapse in several experimental settings but pulled long-run behavior toward the verifier’s knowledge center. The verifier becomes a ceiling and a bias source. Maintain verifier-disagreement slices and human or environment-grounded checks.
The “data wall” is better treated as a constraint curve than a calendar deadline. Unique, eligible, high-quality tokens in a target distribution are finite; extraction, multilingual sources, multimodal transcription, licensed collections, domain partnerships, repetition, and synthetic transformations change the available supply and its cost. A model can consume more tokens than exist uniquely by repeating data, but tokens seen are not unique information. Track marginal gain per additional unique token and per repeat. When the same documents recur, randomized order does not make them new evidence.
Synthetic data is most defensible when it supplies a known missing intervention—worked examples, rare failure cases, executable programs, counterfactual environments—and when acceptance is measured against an authentic target. It is least defensible when a generator fills a volume quota and average loss is the only guardrail.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.13 Landscape 2026 (dated)
The durable spine above remains fitting, controlled data experiments, provenance, and held-out evaluation. The examples below show how those mechanisms are currently composed; they are not universal recipes.






Note



Verify live: 2026-07-19. The OLMo 3 technical report, v2 dated 2026-04-14 describes a staged base-model program with up to 5.9 trillion broad-pretraining tokens, a 100-billion-token mid-training stage, then model-size-dependent long-context stages. Its standard microanneal compares a candidate source plus web text against a compute-matched web-only control, promotes candidates to larger integration tests, retains natural web/PDF anchors to limit distribution shift, and evaluates long context with more than one suite. These are current implementation choices, not constants.
A 2026 analysis of IsoFLOP fitting reports that a common parabolic fitting approach can be biased by finite observations and noise. The current action is durable: fit multiple ways, hold out runs, report uncertainty, and treat method disagreement as a planning risk.
The moving frontier now treats reinforcement-learning compute and test-time compute as additional scaling axes. Those mechanisms are quarantined to Chapters 8 and 23 rather than mixed into this dense-pretraining fit. Current evidence also contradicts the slogan that “pretraining is over”: staged acquisition, mixture search, mid-training, context extension, and synthetic-data verification remain active base-model engineering programs.
An ICLR 2026 synthetic-data study reports that external verification can prevent collapse and produce near-term gains in its regression, VAE, and small-language-model settings, while long-run behavior moves toward verifier bias. Recheck scope before generalizing to frontier training.

5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.14 Build
Run the integrated build from the newbook directory:
python code/ch05/run_build.py
pytest -q tests/test_ch05_scaling_pretraining.py
The default command creates a temporary WET-style file of at least 50 MiB inside the output directory, processes it, then removes the raw fixture. The committed outputs are small: metrics.json, six CSV ledgers including document-ledger.csv, samples.txt, and three SVG figures. No network or GPU is required.
The scaling side reads proxy_ladder.csv, fits the five parameters, checks every residual, and residual-bootstraps the tenfold forecast. The checked-in run recovers \alpha=0.332 and \beta=0.287. Its normalized coefficients, floor, fit interval, predictive interval, and residual-model limitation are recorded rather than rounded into the code. The experiment contract in metrics.json states that the data are synthetic.
The corpus side generated 885 records. Of those, 354 passed rights and quality gates, 265 remained after near-deduplication, 264 remained after removing the planted evaluation substring, and the exact 50/30/20 source mixture selected 60 documents. Inspect pipeline-stages.csv, dedup-clusters.csv, and the document ledger; every extracted ID retains URL, source, language, rights, content hash, policy version, cluster exemplar, final decision, and reason.
Each TinyGPT has 22,640 parameters. Within each of three paired seeds, raw and cleaned conditions share the initial state, optimizer, batch size, context length, and 80 update steps. Their tokenizer is frozen from independent calibration text, and the held-out facts and sentence template do not occur in either sampled training condition. Mean held-out loss is {3.796} for raw and {3.672} for cleaned. Raw-minus-cleaned improvement averages {0.124} nats/token; a paired-seed 90 percent bootstrap interval is [0.056,0.192], and cleaned wins in all three seeds. Both mean cloze accuracies are {0.60} against {0.40} constant-position and shortest-candidate baselines, so the loss result does not establish a qualitative capability difference. Read training-curves.csv, reproducibility hashes and runtime settings in metrics.json, and the saved completions. This fixture validates a controlled intervention; it does not estimate the gain from a named public corpus.
Deliberately break the build in three ways. Set all source weights to web volume and watch the curated domains disappear. Lower the LSH candidate recall by using too many rows per band and inspect missed planted duplicates. Add the evaluation needle after decontamination and observe that a one-time gate cannot protect downstream synthetic transforms. Each break should change a ledger or assertion before it changes a headline score.
The focused tests enforce the chapter’s claims: exponent recovery, exact compute constraint, ordered forecast interval, extraction metadata, rejection reasons, near-duplicate preservation, decontamination, mixture quotas, multilingual fertility, build outputs, and lower clean-held-out loss for the cleaned condition. This is the minimum evidence required to promote either the scaling surface or data recipe to a larger proxy ladder.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.15 What Endures, What Changes
The durable ideas are empirical allocation, not a sacred ratio; joint measurement of model and data; multiple fitting views; explicit uncertainty; tokenizer- and corpus-controlled base evaluation; immutable held-out slices; provenance through every transformation; separate filtering, deduplication, and decontamination; mixture weights as optimized policy; fertility as compute allocation; staged mid-training with controls; and authentic anchors for synthetic feedback loops.
The values will change. Fitted exponents and floors change with architecture, optimizer, precision, corpus, and regime. The serving demand that justifies overtraining changes with products and hardware. Quality classifiers, public corpora, licenses, opt-out mechanisms, model reports, context lengths, and synthetic generators change. Current mid-training token counts and mixture ratios are examples, not defaults.
When those values move, rerun the ladder and pipeline rather than editing a constant in prose. The next chapter takes the budget, data manifest, checkpoints, and in-flight evaluation contract produced here and turns them into a training run.

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.16 Exercises

Stress the extrapolation. Refit the law after dropping the two largest proxy runs. Predict the original tenfold target, compare it with the full-ladder prediction and interval, and explain how much error came from extrapolation rather than optimizer noise.
Price two allocations. Compute tokens needed for a three-billion-parameter model under the fitted compute-optimal allocation and under a ten-times-overtrained allocation. Convert both to FLOPs, MFU-adjusted accelerator-hours, and cost using a dated current $/GPU-hour rate card. State hardware, MFU, and which costs the estimate omits.
Expose filter bias. Add a rare-language slice to the synthetic WET fixture. Design a filter that raises average quality score while deleting much of that slice; quantify retention, tokens, and held-out loss by language. Then propose a gate that makes the failure visible without forcing every domain to identical statistics.
Measure contamination inflation. Insert the toy evaluation prompts and answers into a controlled fraction of training documents, bypass decontamination, and retrain under equal steps. Measure cloze and held-out changes as contamination rises. Distinguish memorization from legitimate general-domain improvement.
Design a mid-training curriculum. Propose three base-model stages for a code-assistant model. Specify source mixture, unique and repeated tokens, sequence-length distribution, learning-rate intervention, control branch, promotion metric, general-domain retention gate, and why each stage belongs before post-training.
Defend a licensed-data purchase. Use the power-law and Chinchilla math to argue for or against paying for a licensed high-quality corpus instead of acquiring more crawl. Include unique-token yield, expected surface shift, rights and deletion costs, proxy-run design, inference lifetime, and the evidence that would reverse your decision.

title: 5  Scaling Laws and Pretraining Data [F+S]
text:
5.17 Notes and Sources
The scaling-law spine comes from Kaplan et al., Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models and Hoffmann et al., Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models. Extensions and cautions include inference-aware scaling, data-constrained scaling, precision-aware scaling, distillation scaling, and MoE scaling. The emergence discussion uses Wei et al. and the metric critique by Schaeffer et al.. Base-evaluation setup sensitivity follows the Language Model Evaluation Harness lessons.
For acquisition, see the Common Crawl file-format documentation and FAQ and the Data Provenance Initiative. Corpus history and curation evidence come from T5/C4, the C4 audit, The Pile, RedPajama, Dolma, FineWeb, DCLM, and Nemotron-CC.
Deduplication and memorization sources are Lee et al., SemDeDup, Kandpal et al., and Carlini et al.. Mixture and fertility evidence comes from DoReMi, RegMix, and Ahia et al. on language tokenization cost.
For staged training, see domain-adaptive continued pretraining, OLMo 2, scaling laws for data-source utility, long-context data engineering, and ProLong. Synthetic-data claims are grounded in Shumailov et al., Gerstgrasser et al., and WRAP. Current examples remain isolated in the dated landscape box.

6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.3 A Run That Fits Still May Not Train
text:
6.3 A Run That Fits Still May Not Train
A team’s parameter files fit across its accelerators, so the capacity review passes. The first step fails when Adam state initializes. A second layout waits on an all-to-all across a slow fabric. A third loses forty minutes per host failure, then resumes with a data cursor inconsistent with its optimizer step. Each failure comes from treating distributed training as “put the model on more GPUs.”
The real objective is productive learning per unit of wall time and capital. A valid training design must satisfy several ledgers simultaneously: persistent model state, peak activations, temporary collective buffers, communication volume, numerical error, failure waste, power, and money. Model FLOPs are necessary work, but the system only makes progress when those FLOPs update the intended parameters on the intended tokens and leave a restartable state.
We begin with bytes on one rank, then shard state and computation along explicit device-mesh dimensions. We treat precision and checkpointing as measured policies, connect optimizer parameterization to proxy transfer, and replace one utilization percentage with goodput, recovery, and cost. By the end, you can turn a proposed run into a memory ledger, parallelism map, checkpoint plan, and economic sensitivity analysis.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
text:
6.4 What you will build






Tip



The chapter artifact. The integrated laboratory reuses Chapter 2’s TinyGPT, launches a real fully sharded training job, and measures local parameter, gradient, and optimizer-state bytes. Freshly initialized checkpoint-off and checkpoint-on phases use identical weights, optimizer state, RNG, and batches; the probe compares final loss and gradient norm, counts tensors saved by autograd, records tokens per second, and computes a labeled local-roofline MFU proxy. A seeded optimizer probe compares AdamW with a small Muon-like matrix update under one warmup-stable-decay schedule. A formula-driven XLSX cost workbook and CSV model translate a hypothetical 30-billion-parameter, 600-billion-token dense run into FLOPs, accelerator-hours, wall time, power, energy, and cost across an MFU sweep.
Success means actual distributed tensors and collectives execute; the measured sharded state agrees with the byte ledger; checkpointing saves activation bytes; all optimizer losses remain finite; and every plotted number exists in CSV or JSON. The optimizer curves are a mechanism probe, not a speedrun ranking. The CPU MFU denominator is a measured local matrix-multiplication roofline, not a vendor peak. The dollar and power inputs are illustrative assumptions, not quotes.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.5 A Training Step Has a Byte Ledger
text:
6.5 A Training Step Has a Byte Ledger
Start with a classic mixed-precision Adam estimate. For each trainable parameter, store a two-byte BF16 parameter, a two-byte gradient, a four-byte FP32 master copy, and two four-byte FP32 moments. The persistent model-state total is therefore approximately

M_{\text{Adam}} = P(2+2+4+8)=16P\ \text{bytes},

where P is the number of trainable parameters. Other component choices can yield the same total. BF16-native updates, quantized moments, fused layouts, factored statistics, or Muon state require a different ledger.
Sixteen bytes per parameter is a conventional mixed-precision Adam baseline, not a lower bound or peak-memory forecast. Activations depend on microbatch size, sequence length, width, depth, attention algorithm, and checkpoint policy. Temporary memory includes an all-gathered parameter unit, reduction buckets, attention workspaces, logits, allocator fragmentation, and framework metadata. Some bytes persist; others overlap briefly. An out-of-memory incident depends on the maximum simultaneous set, so a design review asks for a timeline as well as a total.
Ordinary distributed data parallelism (DDP) gives every rank a full copy of the model and optimizer state. Each rank processes a different microbatch, computes local gradients, and participates in a gradient all-reduce so all replicas apply the same update. In an ideal ring all-reduce over p ranks, reducing a gradient payload of S bytes moves approximately

V_{\text{rank}} \approx \frac{2(p-1)}{p}S

bytes per rank. A latency-aware estimate adds \,2(p-1)\alpha, where \alpha is per-hop startup latency. Libraries bucket gradients and overlap reductions with backward, so readiness and message size matter alongside total bytes.
Gradient accumulation delays synchronization while one rank processes several microbatches. For fixed-size sequences,

B_{\text{global}}=B_{\text{micro}}\,a\,p_{\text{DP}},

where B_{\text{micro}} is examples per rank per forward pass, a is the number of accumulated microbatches, and p_{\text{DP}} is the data-parallel degree. Variable-length training should record non-padding tokens instead of examples. Accumulation lowers communication frequency and can make a model fit, but it retains the activation cost of only one microbatch while increasing update latency.
The critical batch size is the regime beyond which more examples per update yield sharply diminishing reductions in gradient noise. It changes with training phase, data, optimizer, and learning rate. Measure loss versus seen tokens and wall time across a batch ladder. If doubling hardware barely reduces steps to target loss, the extra replicas bought utilization rather than learning speed.
memory_math.py makes the steady-state components and transient largest-unit parameters explicit. Its communication estimate is deliberately a ring-equivalent byte model, not a network simulator.








Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




memory_math.py ledger
153 lines
DDP holds \,16P modeled bytes; each ZeRO stage shards only its promised state



Name every byte by owner, lifetime, dtype, and shard group. “The model fits” is not a memory invariant.

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.6 Shard Model State with ZeRO and FSDP
text:
6.6 Shard Model State with ZeRO and FSDP
DDP stops scaling when replicated state consumes each rank. Zero Redundancy Optimizer (ZeRO) removes one class of replication at each stage. Let p_s be the state-sharding degree and retain the 16-byte Adam convention. Ignoring rounding and transient buffers, the per-rank persistent state is approximately:










Strategy
Parameters
Gradients
Master + moments
Per-rank total




DDP / stage 0
\,2P
\,2P
\,12P
\,16P


ZeRO stage 1
\,2P
\,2P
\,12P/p_s
\,4P+12P/p_s


ZeRO stage 2
\,2P
\,2P/p_s
\,12P/p_s
\,2P+14P/p_s


ZeRO stage 3 / fully sharded
\,2P/p_s
\,2P/p_s
\,12P/p_s
\,16P/p_s



Stage 1 shards optimizer state. Stage 2 also shards gradients; stage 3 shards parameters between computations. Its attractive \,16P/p_s term does not let a layer execute from arbitrary fragments. Before a sharded module computes, its parameter group is gathered into the required layout and later resharded. Peak memory includes the largest materialized unit, activations, and collective workspaces.
Granularity matters. One root-sized unit creates a huge blocking all-gather. Transformer-block units expose prefetch and reshard overlap; units that are too small pay excessive startup and bookkeeping. Measure boundaries against layer size, fabric latency, prefetch depth, and peak memory.
A distributed tensor pairs one logical global tensor with a device mesh and shard, replicate, or partial placements. A matrix may be output-sharded for tensor parallelism while its state is sharded on a data-parallel dimension. Operators propagate compatible layouts or redistribute. Every layout transformation must remain accountable.
Hybrid sharded data parallelism (HSDP) shards within a group and replicates shards across groups. With p_s=8 rack-local ranks and r=16 replicas, each rank retains about \,16P/8, not \,16P/128. Latency-sensitive gather and scatter stay inside eight ranks; replica synchronization crosses the slower boundary.
Sharding changes checkpoints too. A local rank shard is not a model checkpoint. A distributed writer should stage rank-local shards without blocking every training step, record global tensor names, shapes, placements, optimizer state, and checksums in a reshardable manifest, flush to durable storage, validate completeness, then atomically publish a commit marker. Bound staging memory and bandwidth with backpressure; never expose a partial save. Recovery should reconstruct logical tensors on a different world size, not depend on old rank identities.
The central trade is not “memory versus communication” in the abstract. It is persistent replication versus timed materialization across a particular collective group. A useful plan lists steady-state bytes, largest gathered unit, collective type and message size, and whether communication can overlap useful computation.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.7 Compose Tensor, Pipeline, Context, and Expert Parallelism
text:
6.7 Compose Tensor, Pipeline, Context, and Expert Parallelism
State sharding alone does not divide the computation of a layer. When one rank cannot execute a layer quickly or fit its transient tensors, add model-parallel axes. Each axis partitions a different mathematical object and therefore introduces a different collective.
Tensor parallelism (TP) partitions matrices inside a layer. Column-parallel linears split output features; row-parallel linears consume split inputs and sum partial outputs. Alternating layouts can avoid gathers, but reductions sit on nearly every layer’s critical path. TP belongs on fast scale-up links, and excessive TP leaves kernels too small to saturate devices.
Pipeline parallelism (PP) partitions depth into s sequential stages. Microbatches flow through the stages so forward and backward work overlaps. For balanced stages and a simplified one-forward/one-backward schedule, the fill-and-drain bubble fraction is

f_{\text{bubble}}\approx\frac{s-1}{m+s-1},

where m is microbatches per optimizer step. Eight stages and 32 microbatches give \,7/39\approx17.9\% idle bubble before imbalance and communication. More microbatches reduce it but add overhead and may alter global batch. Interleaved virtual stages avoid multiplying the batch but add transfers and scheduling complexity.
Context parallelism (CP) partitions sequence positions. Ring Attention keeps a query block local, circulates key/value blocks, and incrementally combines exact attention statistics. Ulysses-style parallelism all-to-all transposes sequence shards into head shards, computes full-sequence attention for subsets of heads, then reverses the transpose. Ring exposes point-to-point overlap; Ulysses needs head divisibility and strong all-to-all. The system question is whether communication fits the topology and hides behind compute.
Expert parallelism (EP) places different mixture-of-experts feed-forward networks on different ranks. Routing becomes a dispatch all-to-all before expert compute and a combine all-to-all after it. A simple dispatch-plus-combine payload estimate is

V_{\text{EP}}\approx2Tdkbf,

where T is routed tokens in the group, d is hidden width, k is experts selected per token, b is bytes per activation scalar, and f is the fraction assigned off-rank. It omits metadata, padding, quantization scales, congestion, and load imbalance. A dropless router preserves every assignment but lets the most loaded expert set step time unless kernels and routing rebalance work. Static expert capacity bounds time and memory but may drop or reroute tokens, changing the model computation.
An existing dense checkpoint can be upcycled into an MoE by copying feed-forward weights into experts, initializing routers, and continuing training. Duplicated experts initially lack specialization and routing can destabilize optimization, so compare with a from-scratch compute-matched control.
These dimensions are not automatically independent. A convenient first sketch is

p_{\text{world}}=p_{\text{DP}}p_{\text{TP}}p_{\text{PP}}p_{\text{CP}},

but EP may reuse a process-group dimension rather than multiply world size. Draw rank sets: one rank can join TP for attention, EP for feed-forward dispatch, a PP edge, and a sharded-data group. Unmapped labels produce impossible counts and double-counted bandwidth.

  end

The textual invariant in Figure 6.1 is that DP, TP, PP, and CP identify orthogonal partitions only when their rank sets are actually orthogonal; EP is an overlay until the process-group map proves it is an additional physical dimension. The diagram is a logical map, not a claim that eight devices suit a real model.

6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.8 Trade Precision and Recomputation for Capacity
text:
6.8 Trade Precision and Recomputation for Capacity
Precision is a policy over tensors and operations. BF16 keeps an FP32-like exponent range with fewer significand bits, usually avoiding the dynamic-range failures that make FP16 loss scaling necessary. Reductions, normalization, optimizer state, attention logits, and softmax accumulation may still need FP32; gradient communication may use another dtype. Record each choice.
FP8 adds scaling. E4M3 favors precision; E5M2 favors range. Specify tensor eligibility, scale granularity and updates, accumulator dtype, clipping, rounding, and high-precision islands. “FP8 training” never means every operation is FP8. Inspect overflow, underflow, saturation, scales, gradients, attention-logit tails, and agreement with a higher-precision reference.
Activation checkpointing attacks a different term. Autograd normally stores intermediate tensors during forward so backward can reuse them. Checkpointing saves selected boundary tensors and recomputes the enclosed forward region during backward. It exchanges extra FLOPs for lower peak activation memory. Transformer-block granularity is a robust starting point: checkpoint every block, measure peak allocated bytes and tokens per second, then selectively retain expensive-to-recompute regions if memory allows.
Recomputation must be semantically identical. Dropout requires consistent RNG state; stateful modules, mutable caches, or side effects can change gradients. Compare one step from identical parameters and inputs, check finite gradients, and bound differences under the chosen precision.

sequenceDiagram
  participant R0 as Rank 0
  participant R1 as Rank 1
  participant A as Autograd
  R0->>R1: all-gather block parameter shards
  R1->>R0: all-gather block parameter shards
  Note over R0,R1: full block parameters are transient
  R0->>R0: forward on local microbatch
  R1->>R1: forward on local microbatch
  R0-->>A: save block boundary only
  R1-->>A: save block boundary only
  Note over R0,R1: reshard parameters after forward
  A->>R0: backward requests block
  A->>R1: backward requests block
  R0->>R1: all-gather, recompute, differentiate
  R1->>R0: all-gather, recompute, differentiate
  R0->>R1: reduce-scatter gradient shards
  R1->>R0: reduce-scatter gradient shards

  participant R0 as Rank 0
  participant R1 as Rank 1
  participant A as Autograd
  R0->>R1: all-gather block parameter shards
  R1->>R0: all-gather block parameter shards

  R0->>R0: forward on local microbatch
  R1->>R1: forward on local microbatch

  R1-->>A: save block boundary only

  A->>R1: backward requests block

  R1->>R0: all-gather, recompute, differentiate
  R0->>R1: reduce-scatter gradient shards
  R1->>R0: reduce-scatter gradient shards




Figure 6.2: A sharded, checkpointed block: when do full parameters and activations exist on each rank?





Figure 6.2 explains why multiplying “sharded parameter bytes” by a safety factor is not enough. Full unit parameters reappear during compute, checkpointed activations disappear and later reappear through recomputation, and gradient shards emerge only after a collective. Prefetch can overlap these events and increase the simultaneous peak.






Figure 6.3: A two-panel locally measured figure. The left panel stacks predicted state bytes for DDP and three sharding stages. The right panel compares autograd-saved tensor bytes without and with block checkpointing.



In the seeded Chapter 2 model, checkpointing reduced tensors saved by autograd from 3,130,372 to 610,052 bytes, an 80.5 percent reduction. The two-rank CPU phase took longer with checkpointing in the recorded run, as recomputation predicts, but the tiny timing is environment-specific and noisy. The durable conclusion from Figure 6.3 is the byte trade, not its absolute speed.

New code
Invariant now verified




probe_model.py plus train_dist.py

block checkpointing preserves a valid backward pass while reducing saved tensors



Use low precision to reduce storage, bandwidth, and arithmetic cost; use checkpointing to reduce saved activations. They compose, but their error and recomputation effects must be measured separately.

6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.9 Optimizers and Schedules Are Scaling Interfaces
text:
6.9 Optimizers and Schedules Are Scaling Interfaces
AdamW is a strong reference: it maintains exponential first and second moments, divides the first by the square root of the second, and applies decoupled weight decay. A new optimizer must beat a tuned control at equal tokens, active FLOPs, batch, precision, data order, and architecture—not merely equal steps.
Muon takes a different view of two-dimensional hidden weight matrices. It forms a momentum matrix and approximately orthogonalizes the update, commonly through a few Newton–Schulz iterations. In schematic form,

B_t=\mu B_{t-1}+G_t,\qquad O_t\approx\operatorname{NS}_k\!\left(\frac{B_t}{\lVert B_t\rVert_F}\right),

where G_t is the current gradient matrix, \mu is momentum, \lVert\cdot\rVert_F is the Frobenius norm, and \operatorname{NS}_k denotes k approximate orthogonalization iterations. Embeddings, biases, normalization vectors, and sometimes input/output projections remain on AdamW. Update scaling, weight decay, distributed implementation, and precision are part of the recipe; “replace Adam with Muon” is underspecified. Shampoo also preconditions matrix gradients from second-order statistics, while SOAP combines Shampoo-like preconditioning with Adam-style updates; both introduce state and distributed-preconditioner costs that need their own ledger.
The maximal update parameterization, or \muP, uses width-dependent initialization, learning-rate, and output scalings so many proxy-model hyperparameters remain useful at larger width. Transfer assumes the same parameterization, architecture family, and optimizer regime; it does not cover arbitrary data, context, batch, or architecture changes. Verify it on a proxy ladder.
The learning-rate schedule is also a scaling interface. Cosine decay ties the entire curve to a known terminal token budget. Warmup-stable-decay (WSD) raises the rate, holds a long stable trunk, and attaches a final decay. The stable checkpoint can branch into several short decays for different budgets, which is valuable when the final token allocation may change. A branch is not free: each candidate consumes data and compute, and its comparison must preserve the data cursor and evaluation protocol.
The probe cannot establish a winner. Both strategies see identical batches and weights under one WSD curve. AdamW falls from 4.1522 to 1.0701 nats per token; the Muon-like split ends at 1.0690. A short single-seed check can expose divergence, not validate a frontier claim.






Figure 6.4: Training loss for AdamW and a Muon-like matrix optimizer on identical seeded TinyGPT batches, with the shared warmup-stable-decay multiplier on a second axis.



Figure 6.4 uses line style as well as color, so its conclusion survives grayscale: both implementations are finite and learn under the controlled fixture; their tiny final separation is not evidence of general superiority. A real optimizer evaluation needs paired seeds, learning-rate and weight-decay sweeps, equal-token quality, throughput, state bytes, communication, checkpoint compatibility, and stability at the target width.








Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




optimizer_probe.py
137 lines
AdamW and the matrix-update strategy see identical batches and remain finite under one WSD schedule



The durable engineering question is whether an optimizer-schedule-parameterization package transfers useful progress per token and per wall-clock dollar. An optimizer name alone is not an experiment.

6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.10 Compose the Mesh Around the Topology
text:
6.10 Compose the Mesh Around the Topology
A parallelism plan becomes credible only on physical links. A fast scale-up domain usually carries latency-sensitive TP, EP, or CP; scale-out often carries PP, DP, or HSDP replica traffic. Benchmark the actual collective, message size, concurrency, route, and failure domain.
For a collective with n participating ranks and payload S, the shorthand

t_{\text{collective}}\approx a(n)\alpha+b(n)\frac{S}{\beta}

separates startup latency \alpha from effective bandwidth \beta; algorithm-dependent a(n) and b(n) encode rings, trees, hierarchical algorithms, or all-to-all schedules. Peak link bandwidth is not \beta. Congestion, protocol overhead, topology, other collectives, and small-message inefficiency reduce it. Benchmark at the shapes generated by the model, then record p50 and tail latency. Step time follows the slowest rank.
Three reference compositions cover many starting points:








Workload
First composition to test
Main reason to reject it




Model state fits one device
DDP, gradient accumulation, BF16, block checkpointing
replicated Adam state or critical-batch inefficiency


Large dense model in one strong cluster
HSDP plus intra-domain TP; add PP only for depth/peak constraints
TP collectives cross slow links or PP bubbles dominate


Long-context MoE
previous stack plus CP for attention and EP for experts
CP/EP all-to-all contention, routing skew, or too-small kernels



Start with the fewest axes that satisfy memory. Choose TP from layer shape and the scale-up domain. Add PP when stages exceed memory or avoid cross-domain TP; CP for long-sequence work; EP from expert placement and overlap; then DP only while the global batch remains efficient.
Hardware selection is system selection: device memory and bandwidth, fabrics, host paths, storage, checkpoint bandwidth, scheduling, telemetry, firmware, spares, rack power, and cooling all matter. A faster chip can lose time to target behind a weak fabric or slow recovery.
Wide-area or decentralized training changes the algorithm because synchronous collectives cannot tolerate WAN latency and churn. Local-update methods let each island run many inner optimizer steps, then periodically combine parameter deltas through an outer optimizer. Communication falls, but replicas drift; staleness, non-identically distributed data, security, and quorum behavior become optimization variables. Treat these methods as local-SGD families with explicit convergence and failure assumptions, not drop-in replacements for a healthy low-latency cluster.
The topology invariant is: place the most frequent and least hideable communication on the strongest links, and make every cross-domain byte justify itself. A process-group diagram and collective trace should agree.

6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.11 Frontier Stability Is a Goodput Problem
text:
6.11 Frontier Stability Is a Goodput Problem
Model FLOPs utilization (MFU) estimates what fraction of aggregate peak arithmetic performs the model’s nominal forward-and-backward work:

\operatorname{MFU}=\frac{q\,F_{\text{model/token}}}{G P_{\text{peak}}},

where q is observed training tokens per second, F_{\text{model/token}} is the declared model FLOPs per token, G is accelerator count, and P_{\text{peak}} is peak FLOPs per accelerator in the relevant precision. A dense Transformer baseline often uses F_{\text{model/token}}\approx6P, with attention-length corrections. For MoE, use activated parameters or profiler-derived FLOPs. Publish the counting convention, precision, sparsity treatment, and whether recomputation is excluded.
Hardware FLOPs utilization (HFU) counts implementation-executed arithmetic, including recomputation. Checkpointing can raise HFU while leaving model progress unchanged, so HFU is useful for kernel diagnosis but less comparable across implementations. MFU can also hide idle days: it is normally computed while steps execute.
Goodput is useful work completed per wall-clock resource. One component is the effective-training-time rate (ETTR), productive nominal iteration time divided by elapsed wall time. When denominators align, \operatorname{MFU}\times\operatorname{ETTR} is a rough productive fraction of peak capacity. Keep both terms: low MFU points toward kernels, communication, or bubbles; low ETTR points toward failures, checkpoint stalls, data starvation, validation pauses, or scheduler gaps.
Expected time to recovery—also abbreviated ETTR by some teams—is detection, isolation, replacement, checkpoint load, and replay. Spell out the ambiguous acronym. Record interruption rate, tail recovery, lost tokens, automated-handling fraction, and first-step correctness.
A complete checkpoint includes model, optimizer, schedule, scaler, token count, data cursor, per-rank RNG, topology, configuration, tokenizer/data/code digests, and the integrity manifest. A clean-worker, different-world-size restart drill is stronger evidence than a save log.
Checkpoint frequency has an economic optimum. If a synchronous checkpoint costs C_s seconds, job mean time between interrupting failures is M, restart cost is R, and checkpoints occur every I seconds, a simple waste model is

w(I)\approx\frac{C_s}{I}+\frac{I}{2M}+\frac{R}{M}.

The first term is checkpoint overhead; the second is average work lost since the last checkpoint under uniformly timed failures; the third is restart overhead. Minimizing the first two gives Young’s approximation I^*\approx\sqrt{2C_sM}. Correlated rack failures, preemption warnings, asynchronous staging, remote persistence lag, and checkpoint corruption violate the model. Use it to initialize policy, then fit failure and save distributions from operations data.
Silent data corruption (SDC) produces plausible but wrong values. ECC cannot cover every arithmetic, transfer, firmware, or software fault. Layer defenses include burn-in, distribution checks, training monitors, collective probes, checksums, quarantine, replay, and clean restoration. Measure detector coverage and false positives; restarting a corrupt checkpoint only reproduces failure.
Frontier stability is also optimization stability. Classify a spike before responding: data faults include corrupt or shifted batches; numerical faults include overflow, attention-logit growth, and bad scales; routing faults include expert collapse or overload; system faults include corrupted arithmetic, collectives, or state. Preserve batch identifiers, metrics, and state hashes for replay. A skip policy must be explicit, bounded, and logged; nonfinite or corrupted optimizer state requires rollback to a verified checkpoint, not blind continuation. Preventive controls can include gradient clipping, QK-clip or query/key rescaling to cap attention logits, and router z-loss to penalize extreme routing logits. Each changes optimization and needs an ablation. “Loss recovered” is not proof that moments and data order remained valid.
The operating invariant is that a run is healthy only when it makes verifiable forward progress and can recover to the same logical training state. High MFU during surviving minutes does not compensate for low goodput across the month.

6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]

title: 6  Distributed and Frontier Training [F+S]
section: 6.12 Training Economics Converts Utilization into Procurement
text:
6.12 Training Economics Converts Utilization into Procurement
The compute budget from Chapter 5 becomes an elapsed-time model. For a dense Transformer with P parameters and D training tokens,

C_{\text{train}}\approx6PD.

With G accelerators, per-device peak rate F, and MFU u,

T_{\text{seconds}}=\frac{C_{\text{train}}}{GFu},\qquad
H_{\text{accelerator}}=\frac{C_{\text{train}}}{Fu\,3600}.

The second equation is independent of G: ideal scaling changes wall time, not accelerator-hours. Real scaling changes u, batch, ETTR, and often price. For MoE and long context, replace \,6P with activated or profiled FLOPs and include attention’s sequence-dependent work.
Cloud cost begins with H_{\text{accelerator}} times an effective rate. Add storage, transfer, orchestration, support, failed and development runs, idle reservations, and preemption. Spot lowers nominal rates but increases interruption waste; reserved capacity can create utilization risk. Model distributions, not one rate cell.
Power needs an equally explicit boundary. If the combined accelerator-and-node IT draw averages W_{\text{IT}} per accelerator and the facility power-usage effectiveness is PUE,

P_{\text{facility}}\approx G W_{\text{IT}}\operatorname{PUE}.

Energy is power integrated over wall time. Do not multiply a facility-inclusive watt assumption by PUE a second time. Average draw, peak rack provisioning, and grid capacity answer different questions: the energy bill uses the first; cluster feasibility and demand charges care about the others.
Buying capacity replaces the hourly quote with annualized ownership. For discount rate r and useful life L, the capital-recovery factor is

\operatorname{CRF}=\frac{r(1+r)^L}{(1+r)^L-1}.

Divide annualized capex plus energy, facility, network, spares, software, financing, and staff by productive accelerator-hours, with utilization and ETTR in the denominator. A cluster bought for one burst can cost more than cloud despite cheap chip-plus-electricity arithmetic.






Figure 6.5: Illustrative sensitivity of training cost and wall time to MFU while model size, token budget, accelerator peak, count, and hourly rate remain fixed.



The base case behind Figure 6.5 is hypothetical: 30B parameters, 600B tokens, 8,192 accelerators, 1,000 TFLOP/s each, 40 percent MFU, a $4 accelerator-hour, and 1.2 facility-inclusive kW each. It yields \,1.08\times10^{23} FLOPs, 75,000 accelerator-hours, 9.16 hours, $300,000, 9.8304 MW, and 90 MWh. These are fixtures, not a forecast. Moving MFU from 40 to 50 percent multiplies time and compute charge by \,0.4/0.5=0.8.








Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




cost_model.py plus cost-sensitivity.csv
54 lines plus eight auditable rows
compute remains fixed while time and accelerator-hours scale as \,1/u



A rent-versus-buy decision also values time to capacity, topology guarantees, capacity certainty, scaling efficiency, egress, compliance, support, and technology-stranding risk. Ask what happens at twice the nominal dollars per useful FLOP, half the expected utilization, a six-month delivery slip, or correlated failures. Procurement is a distribution over outcomes, not a comparison of one chip price with one cloud rate.
